course_description:
This course deals with the contributions of economic analysis to public policy and governance. The main rationale is to help students better understand how mainstream economists think and how they advise governments and other organisations involved in public policy and broader governance issues. After completing this course students should be able to more fully understand the language and major concepts of mainstream economic analysis as applied to governance and public policy. The course also engages in a critical evaluation of such contributions.

POLS7406 considers the relationship between economic analyses and public policy. The ideas of economists have often been politically influential so POLS7406 considers how mainstream economists think about public policy and why/how they influence policymaking and policy institutions. After introducing some basic economic concepts, the course will explore how these apply to real policy issues. Students will explore how markets work (and fail), why governments intervene, and how economic ideas influence the direction of economic policy across several areas including economic growth, inflation, unemployment, taxation, and finance.
timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
The in-person version of this course comprises 11 two-hour seminars. These sessions are recorded and posted on Blackboard for external students to listen to when they wish. Week 13 will be dedicated to revisions ahead of the oral assessment.
course_aims: This course aims to help students better understand how mainstream economists think and how they advise governments and other organisations involved in public policy and broader governance issues.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically evaluate key economic concepts as they apply to policy making.
Communicate clear and persuasive arguments about the merits of an economic approach to particular policy contexts.
Leverage theoretical frameworks and case studies to evaluate current global economic issues.

assessment_details.title: In-class quizzes (Topics 2-11)
assessment_details.weight: 32% Each quiz is worth 4% of your final grade.
assessment_details.task_description: Quizzes (written in class, on paper) take place at the end of the seminar. Quizzes will consist of short answers to questions regarding the required readings for the same week (i.e. in Week 2 the quiz will ask questions regarding the readings set for Week 2). Since only the top 8 quizzes out of 10 count towards the final mark, allowing students to miss two classes without compromising their final grade, I will not provide alternative assessment unless under exceptional, and well-evidenced circumstances. The quiz will consist of two question, each worth 2 points. The first will ask you to briefly define a key concept, based on one of the required readings. You will be assessed on the accuracy of your definition, based on the particular text. The second question will test your understanding of how the same concept is being used by the author(s) in the text. You will be assessed on how accurately you have used the concept, in line with its usage in the text. No half points will be given, so a score of 2 indicates a mostly or very accurate response to a question, 1 is partly accurate, and 0 is mostly or wholly inaccurate. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students will hand in their quiz papers directly to Prof Hameiri
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Essay (2500 words)
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students will write a 2,500 essay on a question supplied by the course coordinator. The marking criteria will be available in Blackboard. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. While students can use AI tools for labour-saving jobs, such as helping with finding sources or grammar, it is expected that the essay is the student's own work. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin .
In uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission. Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you don’t see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Unless an extension is granted, penalties for late submission apply. Students are penalised 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item for every calendar day that an assessment item is late.
Marks will be deducted each day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point the submitted item will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24-hour block is triggered from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.task_description: Students will complete a 20 minute oral assessment on the course's content. Each student will be asked 2 questions, each worth 14 points for a total of 28, and will have up to 10 minutes to answer each question for a total oral assessment of 20 minutes. Marking criteria for this assessment item will be available in Blackboard. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Topic 2: Thinking like an economist We will provide a basic understanding of the different schools of economic analysis and concepts used within each, explain some of the ways in which economic analysis influences policymaking, and consider some of the problems encountered in, or generated by, mainstream economic analysis.
Topic 3: Markets and Market Failure We will gain a basic understanding of the market mechanism, the different types of market capitalism, and how economists theorise and deal with market failure.
Topic 4: The Economic Role of the State We will gain a basic understanding of how economists view the nature and functions of the state in advanced economies and societies.

Topic 5: State Failure and State Capacity We will further explain rational choice theory and its notion of ‘state failure’. We will also introduce the concept of 'state capacity' as alternative and discuss the debate between optimistic and pessimistic views of the ‘possibilities of politics’ for economic governance.

Topic 6: The Economic Analysis of Institutions We will develop an understanding of the main arguments and perspectives of institutional economic analysis and explore their views on the impact of institutions on economic policy and economies' performance.
Mid-semester break No class this week
Reading week: no class No class this week

Topic 7: Economics and Human Behaviour We will understand mainstream economics' assumptions about human behaviour and how advances in behavioural economics have questioned these assumptions and developed different explanations for the interactions of human behaviour with institutions and the economy.

Topic 8: Taxation We will understand the concepts and ideas that drive debates about unemployment and employment policy, and examine how competing economic perspectives influence policy debates and policy or have been ignored.

Topic 9: Inflation and Unemployment We will examine the nature of macroeconomic policy and its recent dynamics, investigating the interplay between fiscal and monetary policies and the growing challenge of coordinating them.

Topic 10: Banking and finance We will discuss the fundamental principles of banking, consider the global financial crisis as a case study in systemic risk, and reflect on debates over the role of central banking and financial regulation.

Topic 11: Economic Growth We will examine the structural adjustment challenges confronting Australia and the concepts and ideas that drive debates about structural adjustment and industry policy, against the backdrop of the breakdown of economic globalisation.
Revision week Students will have the opportunity for Q&A relating the course's content.
